summary: Gruber announces Dan Benjamin's new venture Hiveware and its first product, Enkoder, a tool to protect email addresses on web pages from spam harvesters. He explains the problem: mailto links expose addresses to spambots crawling the web. While HTML entity encoding offers some protection, it's easily defeated by a few lines of Perl. Enkoder uses JavaScript obfuscation to hide addresses from source code scrapers while keeping them clickable in browsers. Gruber acknowledges it's 'heightened obscurity' rather than impregnable security, but recommends it as a practical step ahead of spammers.
key_points: Putting email addresses in plain text on web pages guarantees spam, as harvesters crawl for mailto links
HTML entity encoding is a rudimentary defense easily defeated by simple Perl scripts
Enkoder uses JavaScript obfuscation to hide addresses from source code while preserving usability in browsers
The tool is available free in three forms: Mac OS X app, web form, and cross-platform command-line tool
money_quotes: You can tell that the new Enkoder is better and more fun, because its name is spelled with a k.
If you put an address on a web page in plain text, soon thereafter you will begin receiving spam at that address.
The problem with this technique is that it isn't very hard at all to write a spam harvesting spider that can decode HTML entities. A few lines of Perl will do it.
This is not impregnable security. It is merely heightened obscurity.
key_insight: Email address protection on the web is an arms race of obscurity rather than true security, but practical JavaScript obfuscation can stay one step ahead of spam harvesters.
